---
title: "We made a map of the doom debate. Here's where everyone actually stands."
date: "June 2026"
author:
  - Sean Herrington
  - Khai Tran
  - David Bravo
  - Kevin Caldwell
  - Paul Hindoian
  - Josh Tuffy
  - Mikaela Cankosyan
  - Christopher A. Davis
  - Maryam Hampaei
---

*This is the third post in a series titled "We made a map of the doom debate," produced as part of the AI Safety Camp 2026 "Assumptions of the Doom Debate" project, led by Sean Herrington. The [first post](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/dZZD5Lviid2mALAZL/we-made-a-map-of-the-doom-debate) introduced the tree; the [second](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/AZrMGwBPMJmAcgqX8/we-made-a-map-of-the-doom-debate-here-s-how-the-breakdown) walked its nodes. This post places the people. The interactive tree is [here](https://lifeuniversesafety.com/doom-assumptions/index.html).*

TL;DR:

- The AI-risk debate mixes two kinds of question: empirical ones evidence can settle, and framing choices it never will. Most "how dangerous is AI" fights are the second kind wearing the first's clothes.
- We place the recurring voices on the tree from Posts 1–2 by the one thing it tracks: the mechanism each actually fears, not their headline number.
- Sorted that way the loud disagreements shrink: Bengio and Yudkowsky are one crux apart, and Critch's 85% *rejects* the very scenario Yudkowsky centers.
- And the map is lopsided: almost everyone piles onto the single "it knew and did it anyway" branch, leaving whole scenarios with no champion.

::: {.callout-note .meta-note collapse="true" title="Editor note (v2, tree-organized)"}
Working version of Post 3, themed to match Posts 1–2. Intro merged to one section; the gallery walks the tree top-down (Non-AI, then AI-Driven and its branches); the cruxes moved to an appendix. The gallery is the focus. Kept alongside the flat-list draft until we settle.
:::

## Why this map exists

::: {.aside .meta-note}
**META:** One long intro — thesis, the inheritance from Posts 1–2, and the single reading rule (sort by mechanism), ending on the payoff. Absorbs the old gallery-lead. Emphasis stays sparing.
:::

The AI debate is confused, and people argue past each other. Two researchers can report the same probability of catastrophe and mean opposite things by it; two who are forty points apart can mean nearly the same. A decade of shared evidence has barely moved anyone, and when evidence stops moving a disagreement, the disagreement was never about evidence.

Every estimate tangles two kinds of question. Some are empirical — does power-seeking come with capability, would a failure show before it was terminal — and evidence settles them. The rest are not: what counts as doom (an AI that quietly succeeds us: catastrophe, or worthy heirs?), over what horizon, whether a well-behaved model is reassurance or camouflage. No experiment touches those; they decide what the experiments mean. Confuse the two and you argue forever.

Posts 1 and 2 built a tree for the empirical questions: does AI raise the danger, through one system or many, does it know what it is doing, does it expect the result. The tree sorts scenarios, not people. What divides people is what it leaves off — the framing questions, a few factual cruxes, and above all what they would do about it — left off on purpose, so everyone can agree on its shape.

So we sort by mechanism: put each figure on the branch they actually expect. When two share a branch and still seem opposed, the difference is not the mechanism but how much weight they give it, what they would do, or what they count as doom — and we say which. Most apparent contradictions dissolve here: people are known for a prescription or a favorite scenario, not for a claim about mechanism, so reading one as a mechanism makes them look like they span the tree. Genuine split belief is rare. Sorted this way the debate shrinks — the sharpest opponents are one crux apart, the firmest alliances share only a conclusion. One note for the gallery below: most views are located, sitting on a branch; a few are distributional, betting the dangerous branches are nearly empty.

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/e-distributional.qmd >}}

## II. AI-Driven Pathway

Worlds where AI raises the danger, in one of two shapes: many systems at once, or a single dominant one.

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/b-distributed.qmd >}}

### II.2 Single dominant AI pathway

One system carries the danger; it splits by whether that system models what it is doing, and, if so, whether it expects the result.

#### II.2.a Internal model pathway

The system knows what it is doing: it understands its actions, understands catastrophe, and can tell when the two meet. It need not be hostile — it can be trying to help and still accept, say, a five-percent chance of catastrophe. What splits the branch is whether it expects its own action to cause the harm.

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/a-knowing-optimizer.qmd >}}

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/c-the-mistake.qmd >}}

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/d-unwitting.qmd >}}

---

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/conclusion.qmd >}}

---

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/cuts.qmd >}}

---

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/sources.qmd >}}
